nodes.text: The Claimant ("Meridian") is a public limited company and operates a chain of approximately 180 retail stores in the United Kingdom together with an online sales channel.
The Defendant ("TechFlow") is a limited company which designs, supplies and implements retail software systems.

By a written Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 ("the MSA"), TechFlow agreed to design, build, configure and implement for Meridian a cloud-based inventory management and electronic point-of-sale platform ("the Platform").
The MSA incorporated a Statement of Work ("the SOW") at Schedule 1, which set out the scope of the works, the implementation plan and the milestones.
The total charges payable under the MSA were £2,400,000 (excluding VAT), payable against milestones.
It was an express term of the MSA and the SOW that the go-live date for the Platform was fixed at 1 October 2024, and that time was of the essence in respect of that date.

At a meeting on or about 5 February 2024, prior to entering into the MSA, TechFlow by its Sales Director Mr Daniel Frost orally represented to Meridian that the Platform would reliably support at least 10,000 concurrent transactions ("the Representation"). Meridian entered into the MSA in reliance upon the Representation. The Representation was false: the Platform was incapable of supporting that volume.
In breach of the MSA, TechFlow delivered the Platform late. The Platform did not go live until 18 November 2024, some seven weeks after the contractual go-live date.
Meridian did not at any time request any change to the agreed scope of the works. All delay was caused by TechFlow’s failure to allocate adequate and competent resources to the project.

On numerous occasions prior to go-live, Meridian warned TechFlow that the Platform was not ready and requested that go-live be deferred. TechFlow ignored those warnings and proceeded to go-live on 18 November 2024.

Following go-live, the Platform was unavailable for more than 40% of trading hours during November and December 2024, causing widespread till failures and an inability to process sales across Meridian’s store estate.

The Platform contained numerous defects, including critical (Severity 1) failures in the stock-synchronisation module which caused stores to display and sell against incorrect stock figures. By reason of those defects the Platform was not of satisfactory quality and was not reasonably fit for its purpose.
Meridian did not accept the Platform or any part of it, and no acceptance or sign-off was given by Meridian at any time.
Further, TechFlow failed to provide adequate training to Meridian’s staff in the use of the Platform.

nodes.label: 15a
nodes.text: By reason of the matters aforesaid Meridian has suffered loss and damage.

nodes.label: 15b
nodes.text: By reason of the matters aforesaid Meridian has suffered loss and damage.
Further, Meridian claims interest on such sums as may be found due, pursuant to section 35A of the Senior Courts Act 1981, at such rate and for such period as the Court thinks fit.

verbatim_texts: The Claimant claims against the Defendant damages for breach of contract and/or negligence and/or misrepresentation arising out of the Defendant’s defective and delayed design, build and implementation of a cloud-based inventory and EPOS platform pursuant to a Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024, together with interest pursuant to section 35A of the Senior Courts Act 1981 and costs.

By a written Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 ("the MSA"), TechFlow agreed to design, build, configure and implement for Meridian a cloud-based inventory management and electronic point-of-sale platform ("the Platform").
The MSA incorporated a Statement of Work ("the SOW") at Schedule 1, which set out the scope of the works, the implementation plan and the milestones.
The total charges payable under the MSA were £2,400,000 (excluding VAT), payable against milestones.
It was an express term of the MSA and the SOW that the go-live date for the Platform was fixed at 1 October 2024, and that time was of the essence in respect of that date.

At a meeting on or about 5 February 2024, prior to entering into the MSA, TechFlow by its Sales Director Mr Daniel Frost orally represented to Meridian that the Platform would reliably support at least 10,000 concurrent transactions ("the Representation").
Meridian entered into the MSA in reliance upon the Representation.
In breach of the MSA, TechFlow delivered the Platform late.

The Supplier shall design, build, configure, test and implement the cloud-based inventory management and EPOS platform ("the Platform") in accordance with the Statement of Work set out at Schedule 1 (the "SOW").
The total charges for the Services are £2,400,000 (excluding VAT), payable against the milestones set out in the SOW and as may be varied by agreement in writing.
The Supplier shall use reasonable endeavours to achieve the target go-live date stated in the SOW.
The detailed implementation plan is a planning estimate and is subject to the change control procedure in clause 3 of the MSA.
Save as varied by this Deed, the MSA remains in full force and effect.
With effect from the date of this Deed, clause 4.1 of the SOW is varied so that the charges are payable: 20% on M1, 20% on M2, 30% on M3 and 30% on M4.
The Loyalty Module was not part of the original scope under SOW-01.

The core build is in good shape and Phase 1 passed the bulk of UAT, but the loyalty module added under Change Order 3 is still being hardened and we have a handful of stock-sync issues we would like to close out under controlled conditions.
I’ll issue Change Order 3 for Priya’s signature to confirm both the scope and the revised date.
Status: Network (Northgate Telecom) — not Platform
Meridian Retail Group plc – Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 – NOTICE OF TERMINATION AND LETTER BEFORE ACTION
We write in connection with your design, build and implementation of the inventory and EPOS platform under the above Agreement ("the MSA").
Our client’s position is that you are in repudiatory breach of the MSA.
Our client accepts your conduct as terminating the MSA with immediate effect and reserves all of its rights.
In any event, clause 14 of the MSA excludes liability for loss of profit and caps our client’s liability at the charges actually paid.
Go-live decision.
The most significant outage, on 25 November 2024, was caused by the failure of your client’s own network provider, Northgate Telecom, and not by the Platform.
I accept this was not in the original Statement of Work.
I signed the Master Services Agreement on Meridian’s behalf on 14 March 2024, and the related Order Form.

This added the customer loyalty module, which Helena Vance had asked TechFlow to provide and which was not in the original Statement of Work, and it revised the go-live date from 1 October to 18 November 2024.
On 12 November 2024 I signed the Phase 1 Acceptance Certificate, confirming that Phase 1 of the platform had passed user acceptance testing.
The application and database remained healthy throughout; the cause was a loss of wide-area network connectivity at the Claimant’s data-centre provider (Northgate Telecom).
I was provided with the Platform application and database logs, the network monitoring data and the defect log for the period 18 November 2024 to 10 January 2025.
Notwithstanding the above, my opinion is that the Platform did contain genuine and serious defects in the stock-synchronisation module (defects D-001 and D-002 in the log).
The pleaded wasted expenditure of £1,800,000 is consistent with the Claimant’s accounting records and the sums invoiced and paid under the Master Services Agreement.

I note, as a matter for the Court rather than for me, that clause 14 of the Master Services Agreement appears to exclude liability for loss of profit and to cap the Supplier’s liability at the charges paid (approximately £1,800,000).

nodes.label: MSA, clause 1.1
nodes.chunk_text: The Supplier shall design, build, configure, test and implement the cloud-based inventory management and EPOS platform ("the Platform") in accordance with the Statement of Work set out at Schedule 1 (the "SOW").

nodes.label: MSA, clause 2.1
nodes.chunk_text: The total charges for the Services are £2,400,000 (excluding VAT), payable against the milestones set out in the SOW and as may be varied by agreement in writing.

nodes.label: MSA, clause 3.1
nodes.chunk_text: The Supplier shall use reasonable endeavours to achieve the target go-live date stated in the SOW. The parties acknowledge that the implementation timetable is a planning estimate and may be adjusted through the change control procedure.

nodes.label: SOW, clause 2.1
nodes.chunk_text: The go-live date of 1 October 2024 is a target date. The detailed implementation plan is a planning estimate and is subject to the change control procedure in clause 3 of the MSA.

nodes.label: SOW, clause 3.2
nodes.chunk_text: Training of the Customer’s store and head-office staff in the use of the Platform is the responsibility of the Customer. The Supplier shall provide a train-the-trainer session and written user guides only.

nodes.label: Deed of Variation No. 1, clause 1
nodes.chunk_text: With effect from the date of this Deed, clause 4.1 of the SOW is varied so that the charges are payable: 20% on M1, 20% on M2, 30% on M3 and 30% on M4.

nodes.label: Deed of Variation No. 1, clause 2
nodes.chunk_text: Save as varied by this Deed, the MSA remains in full force and effect. EXECUTED as a DEED by the parties on the date first above written. For Meridian:	..........................  Priya Nair, Head of Procurement For TechFlow:	..........................  Gregory Manton, Commercial Director

nodes.label: Change Order No. 3, clause 2.1
nodes.chunk_text: The parties agree that, to accommodate the additional works, the go-live date is revised from 1 October 2024 to 18 November 2024. The revised date applies to the Platform as a whole.

nodes.label: UAT Acceptance Certificate, 12 Nov 2024
nodes.chunk_text: This certificate confirms that User Acceptance Testing of Phase 1 of the Platform (core inventory and EPOS) was carried out between 21 October and 11 November 2024. Meridian confirms that Phase 1 has passed User Acceptance Testing and is accepted, subject to the resolution of the outstanding minor (Severity 3 and 4) items listed in the snag log appended to the UAT report. No outstanding Severity 1 or Severity 2 items were recorded at the date of this certificate.

nodes.label: Reilly to Vance, 24 Oct 2024
nodes.chunk_text:
Helena,
Following today’s readiness review, I want to put our recommendation in writing. The core build is in good shape and Phase 1 passed the bulk of UAT, but the loyalty module added under Change Order 3 is still being hardened and we have a handful of stock-sync issues we would like to close out under controlled conditions.
Our clear recommendation is to defer go-live to mid-January 2025, after the peak trading period. Going live on 18 November, immediately before Black Friday and Christmas, carries real risk and removes our contingency. We can hold the 18 November date if Meridian instructs us to, but we would be doing so against our advice.
Please let me know how you wish to proceed.
Marcus Reilly | Project Director | TechFlow Solutions

nodes.label: Vance to Reilly, 24 Oct 2024
nodes.chunk_text:
Marcus,
Thank you, but the board has been clear that we need the new platform live for peak trading — that is the whole point of doing this now. Missing Black Friday and Christmas is not acceptable commercially.
Please proceed to go-live on 18 November 2024 as planned. I understand your team’s reservations and I am content to take that risk on Meridian’s side. Let’s get the remaining stock-sync items onto the snag list and press on.
Helena Vance | Programme Director | Meridian Retail Group

nodes.label: Vance to Reilly, 21 Aug 2024
nodes.chunk_text:
Marcus,
Following the marketing review, the board wants a customer loyalty capability included in the platform before we go live — points accrual, redemption at the till, and a member database. I appreciate this was not in the original SOW, but it is now a priority for us.
Can you scope this as a change and let us have the cost and any timetable impact? We accept there may be a knock-on to the go-live date and we are willing to discuss a revised date to accommodate it.
Helena

nodes.label: Reilly to Vance, 27 Aug 2024
nodes.chunk_text:
Helena,
Thanks. We’ve scoped the loyalty module. The additional cost is £180,000 and, given the additional build and test, we will need to move go-live from 1 October to 18 November 2024. I’ll issue Change Order 3 for Priya’s signature to confirm both the scope and the revised date.
Marcus

nodes.label: Defect Log, entry D-001
nodes.chunk_text: D-001 (19 Nov 2024, Sev 1): Stock-sync: tills show incorrect stock counts; oversell on promotional lines. Status: Platform defect — fixed 31 Dec 2024

nodes.label: Defect Log, entry D-002
nodes.chunk_text: D-002 (22 Nov 2024, Sev 1): Stock-sync: overnight reconciliation job fails intermittently. Status: Platform defect — fixed 2 Jan 2025

nodes.label: Caldwell Pryce LLP, 20 Jan 2025
nodes.chunk_text: Meridian Retail Group plc – Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 – NOTICE OF TERMINATION AND LETTER BEFORE ACTION

nodes.label: Caldwell Pryce LLP, 20 Jan 2025
nodes.chunk_text: We act for Meridian Retail Group plc ("our client"). We write in connection with your design, build and implementation of the inventory and EPOS platform under the above Agreement ("the MSA").

nodes.label: Caldwell Pryce LLP, 20 Jan 2025
nodes.chunk_text: Our client’s position is that you are in repudiatory breach of the MSA. In particular: (i) you delivered the Platform late; (ii) following go-live on 18 November 2024 the Platform suffered serious and repeated failures, including critical defects in the stock-synchronisation module; and (iii) the Platform was not of satisfactory quality nor fit for purpose. These failures occurred during our client’s most important trading period and caused substantial loss.

nodes.label: Harborne Quinn LLP, 7 Feb 2025
nodes.chunk_text: We act for TechFlow Solutions Limited. Your client’s claim is denied in its entirety. Liability and quantum are both disputed.

nodes.label: Harborne Quinn LLP, 7 Feb 2025
nodes.chunk_text: Timetable and scope. The go-live date was a target, not a fixed date. It moved from 1 October to 18 November 2024 by reason of Change Order No. 3, signed by your client on 2 September 2024, which added a customer loyalty module that your client requested and which was not in the original scope. Your client’s assertion that it requested no change to scope is wrong and is contradicted by its own signed Change Order.

nodes.label: Harborne Quinn LLP, 7 Feb 2025
nodes.chunk_text: Go-live decision. Our client expressly recommended, in writing on 24 October 2024, that go-live be deferred to January 2025 to avoid the peak trading period. Your client’s Programme Director rejected that recommendation and instructed our client to proceed on 18 November 2024, accepting the associated risk. Your client cannot now complain of the consequences of its own decision.

nodes.label: Harborne Quinn LLP, 7 Feb 2025
nodes.chunk_text: Availability. The Platform was not "unavailable for more than 40% of trading hours". The most significant outage, on 25 November 2024, was caused by the failure of your client’s own network provider, Northgate Telecom, and not by the Platform.

nodes.label: Harborne Quinn LLP, 7 Feb 2025
nodes.chunk_text: Loss. Your client’s pleaded loss of profit is grossly overstated and ignores (a) the flooding of your client’s Lutterworth distribution centre in December 2024 and (b) the well-documented downturn in the retail sector during that quarter. In any event, clause 14 of the MSA excludes liability for loss of profit and caps our client’s liability at the charges actually paid.

nodes.label: Harborne Quinn LLP, 7 Feb 2025
nodes.chunk_text: Outstanding sums. Your client has wrongly withheld the final two milestone payments totalling £600,000, which remain due to our client. Our client reserves the right to counterclaim.

nodes.label: Vance WS, para 1
nodes.chunk_text: I am Helena Vance, Programme Director at Meridian Retail Group plc. I make this statement from my own knowledge, save where I indicate otherwise. I had overall responsibility for the Platform implementation programme on Meridian’s side.

nodes.label: Vance WS, para 3
nodes.chunk_text: In around August 2024, our marketing team and the board decided we wanted a customer loyalty capability built into the new platform. I asked TechFlow to add a loyalty module. I accept this was not in the original Statement of Work. It was documented in Change Order No. 3, which my colleague Priya Nair signed on 2 September 2024, and which also moved the go-live date to 18 November 2024.

nodes.label: Vance WS, para 4
nodes.chunk_text: As we approached go-live, Marcus Reilly recommended in writing that we delay until January 2025, after the Christmas period. I decided that we should go live on 18 November 2024. The board was firm that we needed the platform live for peak trading, and I took the decision to proceed despite TechFlow’s advice. With hindsight, going live just before Black Friday was optimistic.

nodes.label: Vance WS, para 7
nodes.chunk_text: I considered that the platform we were given was not what we had paid for, and that is why Meridian decided to bring the relationship to an end.

nodes.label: Okafor WS, para 1
nodes.chunk_text: I am Raymond Okafor, Head of Retail Operations at Meridian Retail Group plc. I make this statement from my own knowledge save where otherwise indicated. I am responsible for the running of our store estate.

nodes.label: Okafor WS, para 2
nodes.chunk_text: The new platform went live on 18 November 2024, immediately before our peak trading period. The first few weeks were very difficult for store teams, particularly because of stock figures being wrong at the tills on the new system.

nodes.label: Okafor WS, para 4
nodes.chunk_text: Our trading in December 2024 was poor, but the platform was not the only reason. Our distribution centre at Lutterworth flooded on 3 December 2024 and was effectively out of action for about ten days, which left around 40 stores short of stock on key lines in the first half of December. The market was also weak that quarter, with footfall down across the sector and heavy discounting by competitors.

nodes.label: Nair WS, para 2
nodes.chunk_text: I signed the Master Services Agreement on Meridian’s behalf on 14 March 2024, and the related Order Form. The total contract value was £2.4 million.

nodes.label: Nair WS, para 3
nodes.chunk_text: On 2 September 2024 I signed Change Order No. 3. This added the customer loyalty module, which Helena Vance had asked TechFlow to provide and which was not in the original Statement of Work, and it revised the go-live date from 1 October to 18 November 2024. I understood at the time that we were changing both the scope and the date.

nodes.label: Nair WS, para 4
nodes.chunk_text: On 12 November 2024 I signed the Phase 1 Acceptance Certificate, confirming that Phase 1 of the platform had passed user acceptance testing. There were some minor snags listed, but no critical items were outstanding at that point.

nodes.label: Nair WS, para 5
nodes.chunk_text: In total Meridian paid TechFlow £1.8 million. We withheld the final two milestone payments of £300,000 each — £600,000 in total — because of the problems we experienced after go-live.

nodes.label: Whitfield Expert Report, para 2
nodes.chunk_text: I was provided with the Platform application and database logs, the network monitoring data and the defect log for the period 18 November 2024 to 10 January 2025. Having analysed the logs, my opinion is that the total Platform unavailability attributable to the Platform itself was approximately 6.2% of trading hours over the period.

nodes.label: Whitfield Expert Report, para 3
nodes.chunk_text: That figure is materially lower than the figure of "more than 40% of trading hours" referred to in the pleadings. I have found no support in the logs for a figure of that order.

nodes.label: Whitfield Expert Report, para 5
nodes.chunk_text: Notwithstanding the above, my opinion is that the Platform did contain genuine and serious defects in the stock-synchronisation module (defects D-001 and D-002 in the log). These were correctly categorised as Severity 1. They caused incorrect stock figures at the point of sale and overselling on certain lines, and persisted for approximately six weeks before being resolved.

nodes.label: Whitfield Expert Report, para 7
nodes.chunk_text: In summary: (a) the Platform contained genuine and serious stock-synchronisation defects which fell below the expected standard; but (b) the pleaded allegation that the Platform was unavailable for more than 40% of trading hours is not supported by the evidence, and the largest single outage was caused by the Claimant’s own network provider rather than the Platform.

nodes.label: Greenhalgh Expert Report, para 2
nodes.chunk_text: The pleaded wasted expenditure of £1,800,000 is consistent with the Claimant’s accounting records and the sums invoiced and paid under the Master Services Agreement. I have no reason to challenge this element on a wasted-expenditure basis.

nodes.label: Greenhalgh Expert Report, para 6
nodes.chunk_text: I note, as a matter for the Court rather than for me, that clause 14 of the Master Services Agreement appears to exclude liability for loss of profit and to cap the Supplier’s liability at the charges paid (approximately £1,800,000). If that clause is effective, it would significantly affect the recoverability of the loss-of-profit claim regardless of my quantification.

nodes.text: The Claimant ("Meridian") is a public limited company and operates a chain of approximately 180 retail stores in the United Kingdom together with an online sales channel.
The Defendant ("TechFlow") is a limited company which designs, supplies and implements retail software systems.

By a written Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 ("the MSA"), TechFlow agreed to design, build, configure and implement for Meridian a cloud-based inventory management and electronic point-of-sale platform ("the Platform").
The MSA incorporated a Statement of Work ("the SOW") at Schedule 1, which set out the scope of the works, the implementation plan and the milestones.
The total charges payable under the MSA were £2,400,000 (excluding VAT), payable against milestones.
It was an express term of the MSA and the SOW that the go-live date for the Platform was fixed at 1 October 2024, and that time was of the essence in respect of that date.

At a meeting on or about 5 February 2024, prior to entering into the MSA, TechFlow by its Sales Director Mr Daniel Frost orally represented to Meridian that the Platform would reliably support at least 10,000 concurrent transactions ("the Representation"). Meridian entered into the MSA in reliance upon the Representation. The Representation was false: the Platform was incapable of supporting that volume.
In breach of the MSA, TechFlow delivered the Platform late. The Platform did not go live until 18 November 2024, some seven weeks after the contractual go-live date.
Meridian did not at any time request any change to the agreed scope of the works. All delay was caused by TechFlow’s failure to allocate adequate and competent resources to the project.

On numerous occasions prior to go-live, Meridian warned TechFlow that the Platform was not ready and requested that go-live be deferred. TechFlow ignored those warnings and proceeded to go-live on 18 November 2024.

Following go-live, the Platform was unavailable for more than 40% of trading hours during November and December 2024, causing widespread till failures and an inability to process sales across Meridian’s store estate.

The Platform contained numerous defects, including critical (Severity 1) failures in the stock-synchronisation module which caused stores to display and sell against incorrect stock figures. By reason of those defects the Platform was not of satisfactory quality and was not reasonably fit for its purpose.
Meridian did not accept the Platform or any part of it, and no acceptance or sign-off was given by Meridian at any time.
Further, TechFlow failed to provide adequate training to Meridian’s staff in the use of the Platform.

nodes.label: 15a
nodes.text: By reason of the matters aforesaid Meridian has suffered loss and damage.

nodes.label: 15b
nodes.text: By reason of the matters aforesaid Meridian has suffered loss and damage.
Further, Meridian claims interest on such sums as may be found due, pursuant to section 35A of the Senior Courts Act 1981, at such rate and for such period as the Court thinks fit.

edges.verbatim_quote: Meridian Retail Group plc
edges.reason: The 'plc' designation in the company name directly confirms that Meridian is a public limited company, which is part of the proposition.

edges.verbatim_quote: Meridian Retail Group plc
edges.reason: This quote directly confirms that Meridian is a public limited company, which is a key part of the proposition.

edges.verbatim_quote: Meridian Retail Group plc.
edges.reason: This quote explicitly states that Meridian is a 'plc', which directly confirms the proposition's assertion that Meridian is a public limited company.

edges.verbatim_quote: your design, build and implementation of the inventory and EPOS platform
edges.reason: This quote directly states that the recipient (the defendant) performs design, build, and implementation of retail software systems, which directly supports the activities described in the proposition.

edges.verbatim_quote: TechFlow Solutions Limited
edges.reason: This quote directly identifies the defendant as 'TechFlow Solutions Limited', which confirms the proposition that TechFlow is a limited company.

edges.verbatim_quote: User Acceptance Testing of Phase 1 of the Platform (core inventory and EPOS) was carried out between 21 October and 11 November 2024. Meridian confirms that Phase 1 has passed User Acceptance Testing and is accepted
edges.reason: This quote indicates that a platform matching the description in the proposition is being developed and accepted, which is consistent with an agreement to implement it, although it does not explicitly mention the Master Services Agreement or TechFlow's specific role.

edges.verbatim_quote: Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024
edges.reason: This quote directly confirms the existence and date of the Master Services Agreement as described in the proposition.

edges.verbatim_quote: We write in connection with your design, build and implementation of the inventory and EPOS platform under the above Agreement ("the MSA").
edges.reason: The quote explicitly refers to an "MSA" under which the recipient (implicitly TechFlow) undertook the "design, build and implementation of the inventory and EPOS platform" for Meridian, directly confirming the existence of such an agreement and the services provided.

edges.verbatim_quote: I signed the Master Services Agreement on Meridian’s behalf on 14 March 2024
edges.reason: This quote directly confirms the existence of a Master Services Agreement, its signing date, and Meridian's involvement, which are key factual assertions within the proposition.

edges.verbatim_quote: the sums invoiced and paid under the Master Services Agreement
edges.reason: This quote indicates that a Master Services Agreement existed and was acted upon, which is consistent with the proposition that such an agreement was made.

edges.verbatim_quote: in accordance with the Statement of Work set out at Schedule 1 (the "SOW")
edges.reason: The quote directly confirms that the Statement of Work, referred to as "the SOW," is set out at Schedule 1 and governs the execution of the works, thereby supporting its incorporation and role in defining the scope.

edges.verbatim_quote: The Supplier shall use reasonable endeavours to achieve the target go-live date stated in the SOW. The parties acknowledge that the implementation timetable is a planning estimate and may be adjusted through the change control procedure.
edges.reason: The quote refers to a 'target go-live date stated in the SOW' and an 'implementation timetable,' which is consistent with the SOW setting out an implementation plan and milestones.

edges.verbatim_quote: The detailed implementation plan is a planning estimate and is subject to the change control procedure in clause 3 of the MSA.
edges.reason: This quote, originating from the Statement of Work, directly discusses the detailed implementation plan and its connection to the MSA, thereby supporting the proposition that the SOW set out the implementation plan and was incorporated into the MSA.

edges.verbatim_quote: I appreciate this was not in the original SOW
edges.reason: This quote indicates the existence of an 'original SOW' that defined the scope of work, which is consistent with the proposition that an SOW existed and set out the scope of works.

edges.verbatim_quote: The total charges for the Services are £2,400,000 (excluding VAT), payable against the milestones set out in the SOW and as may be varied by agreement in writing.
edges.reason: This quote directly states the total charges and the payment method, which precisely matches the proposition.

edges.verbatim_quote: the charges are payable: 20% on M1, 20% on M2, 30% on M3 and 30% on M4.
edges.reason: This quote explicitly states that charges are payable against specific milestones, directly confirming the 'payable against milestones' aspect of the proposition.

edges.verbatim_quote: charges paid (approximately £1,800,000)
edges.reason: The quoted text states an approximate amount for charges paid that is different from the amount specified in the proposition.

edges.verbatim_quote: The go-live date of 1 October 2024 is a target date.
edges.reason: This quote states that the go-live date is a 'target date,' which directly contradicts the proposition that the date was 'fixed' and that 'time was of the essence.'

edges.verbatim_quote: the go-live date is revised from 1 October 2024 to 18 November 2024
edges.reason: This quote directly states that the go-live date was changed from 1 October 2024, which contradicts the proposition that the date was fixed at 1 October 2024.

edges.verbatim_quote: We accept there may be a knock-on to the go-live date and we are willing to discuss a revised date to accommodate it.
edges.reason: This quote directly contradicts the proposition by indicating a willingness to discuss and revise the go-live date, implying it was not fixed and time was not of the essence.

edges.verbatim_quote: we will need to move go-live from 1 October to 18 November 2024
edges.reason: This quote indicates a proposed change to the 1 October go-live date, which directly contradicts the proposition that the date was fixed.

edges.verbatim_quote: The go-live date was a target, not a fixed date. It moved from 1 October to 18 November 2024
edges.reason: The quoted text directly states that the go-live date was a target, not fixed, and that it moved from 1 October, which unambiguously contradicts the proposition that the go-live date was fixed at 1 October.

edges.verbatim_quote: Our client expressly recommended, in writing on 24 October 2024, that go-live be deferred to January 2025 to avoid the peak trading period. Your client’s Programme Director rejected that recommendation and instructed our client to proceed on 18 November 2024, accepting the associated risk.
edges.reason: This quote describes discussions and decisions about deferring or proceeding with go-live on dates after 1 October 2024, which directly contradicts the proposition that 1 October 2024 was a fixed, time-of-the-essence go-live date.

edges.verbatim_quote: which also moved the go-live date to 18 November 2024
edges.reason: This quote directly states that the go-live date was moved, contradicting the proposition that the go-live date was fixed at 1 October 2024.

edges.verbatim_quote: it revised the go-live date from 1 October to 18 November 2024
edges.reason: This quote indicates that the go-live date of 1 October was changed, directly contradicting the proposition that the date was fixed.

edges.verbatim_quote: because of the problems we experienced after go-live
edges.reason: This quote indicates that Meridian experienced issues with the platform after it went live, which is consistent with the proposition that the platform was incapable of supporting the promised transaction volume and the representation was false.

edges.verbatim_quote: Our client’s position is that you are in repudiatory breach of the MSA. In particular: (i) you delivered the Platform late; (ii) following go-live on 18 November 2024
edges.reason: This quote directly states the client's position that the platform was delivered late and specifies the go-live date as 18 November 2024, which directly supports the proposition's claims.

edges.verbatim_quote: The new platform went live on 18 November 2024
edges.reason: This quote directly confirms the specific go-live date of the platform as stated in the proposition.

edges.verbatim_quote: I decided that we should go live on 18 November 2024. The board was firm that we needed the platform live for peak trading, and I took the decision to proceed despite TechFlow’s advice.
edges.reason: The quote states that the claimant decided the 18 November 2024 go-live date despite TechFlow's advice to delay until January 2025, which contradicts the proposition that TechFlow was responsible for the platform being delivered late on that date.

edges.verbatim_quote: the platform we were given was not what we had paid for
edges.reason: This quote directly asserts that TechFlow failed to deliver the agreed-upon platform, which supports the proposition that TechFlow's failures were responsible for project issues.

edges.verbatim_quote: Save as varied by this Deed
edges.reason: This clause explicitly states that the original agreement has been varied, which directly contradicts the proposition that Meridian did not at any time request any change to the agreed scope of the works.

edges.verbatim_quote: the loyalty module added under Change Order 3 is still being hardened
edges.reason: This quote indicates that a loyalty module was added to the scope of works via a Change Order, which directly contradicts the proposition that Meridian did not at any time request any change to the agreed scope of the works.

edges.verbatim_quote: The most significant outage, on 25 November 2024, was caused by the failure of your client’s own network provider, Northgate Telecom, and not by the Platform.
edges.reason: This quote attributes a significant outage, which could cause delay, to Meridian's network provider, directly contradicting the proposition that all delay was caused by TechFlow.

edges.verbatim_quote: it revised the go-live date from 1 October to 18 November 2024. I understood at the time that we were changing both the scope and the date.
edges.reason: This quote indicates that a change in scope led to a revision of the go-live date, directly contradicting the proposition that all delay was caused by TechFlow’s failure to allocate adequate and competent resources.

edges.verbatim_quote: Our client expressly recommended, in writing on 24 October 2024, that go-live be deferred to January 2025 to avoid the peak trading period. Your client’s Programme Director rejected that recommendation and instructed our client to proceed on 18 November 2024, accepting the associated risk.
edges.reason: This quote states that TechFlow (our client) recommended deferral and Meridian (your client) rejected it and instructed TechFlow to proceed, which directly contradicts the proposition that Meridian warned TechFlow and TechFlow ignored those warnings and proceeded.

edges.verbatim_quote: I took the decision to proceed despite TechFlow’s advice.
edges.reason: This quote indicates that Meridian proceeded with go-live despite TechFlow advising against it, which directly contradicts the proposition that TechFlow ignored warnings and proceeded to go-live.

edges.verbatim_quote: it revised the go-live date from 1 October to 18 November 2024
edges.reason: This quote states that the go-live date was formally revised to 18 November, which directly contradicts the proposition's claim that TechFlow ignored requests to defer the go-live date.

edges.verbatim_quote: The Platform was not "unavailable for more than 40% of trading hours".
edges.reason: This quote directly refutes the proposition's claim that the Platform was unavailable for more than 40% of trading hours.

edges.verbatim_quote: my opinion is that the total Platform unavailability attributable to the Platform itself was approximately 6.2% of trading hours over the period.
edges.reason: The expert's opinion states the platform was unavailable for approximately 6.2% of trading hours, which directly contradicts the proposition's claim of more than 40% unavailability.

edges.verbatim_quote: That figure is materially lower than the figure of "more than 40% of trading hours" referred to in the pleadings. I have found no support in the logs for a figure of that order.
edges.reason: The expert report directly states that the actual unavailability figure is materially lower than 40% and that logs do not support the pleaded figure.

edges.verbatim_quote: the pleaded allegation that the Platform was unavailable for more than 40% of trading hours is not supported by the evidence
edges.reason: This quote directly states that the evidence does not support the proposition's claim regarding the platform's unavailability for more than 40% of trading hours.

edges.verbatim_quote: D-001 (19 Nov 2024, Sev 1): Stock-sync: tills show incorrect stock counts; oversell on promotional lines. Status: Platform defect
edges.reason: This quote directly identifies a Severity 1 platform defect in the stock-synchronisation module that caused incorrect stock counts and overselling, aligning perfectly with the proposition.

edges.verbatim_quote: D-002 (22 Nov 2024, Sev 1): Stock-sync: overnight reconciliation job fails intermittently. Status: Platform defect
edges.reason: This quote directly identifies a Severity 1 platform defect within the stock-synchronisation module, which aligns with the proposition's claim of critical failures in that module.

edges.verbatim_quote: the Platform suffered serious and repeated failures, including critical defects in the stock-synchronisation module; and (iii) the Platform was not of satisfactory quality nor fit for purpose.
edges.reason: This quote directly asserts that the Platform had critical defects in the stock-synchronisation module and was not of satisfactory quality or fit for purpose, which directly supports the proposition.

edges.verbatim_quote: the Platform did contain genuine and serious defects in the stock-synchronisation module (defects D-001 and D-002 in the log). These were correctly categorised as Severity 1. They caused incorrect stock figures at the point of sale and overselling on certain lines
edges.reason: This quote directly confirms the existence of Severity 1 defects in the stock-synchronisation module and their effect of causing incorrect stock figures, which are key factual assertions of the proposition.

edges.verbatim_quote: the Platform contained genuine and serious stock-synchronisation defects which fell below the expected standard
edges.reason: The quote directly confirms the existence of genuine and serious stock-synchronisation defects and states they fell below the expected standard, supporting the proposition's claim of defects and unsatisfactory quality.

edges.verbatim_quote: Meridian confirms that Phase 1 has passed User Acceptance Testing and is accepted, subject to the resolution of the outstanding minor (Severity 3 and 4) items listed in the snag log appended to the UAT report.
edges.reason: This quote directly contradicts the proposition by stating that Meridian accepted Phase 1 of the Platform.

edges.verbatim_quote: Please proceed to go-live on 18 November 2024 as planned.
edges.reason: This instruction from a Meridian representative to launch the platform directly contradicts the proposition that Meridian did not accept the platform or give any sign-off.

edges.verbatim_quote: On 12 November 2024 I signed the Phase 1 Acceptance Certificate, confirming that Phase 1 of the platform had passed user acceptance testing.
edges.reason: This quote directly states that an Acceptance Certificate was signed, which contradicts the proposition that no acceptance or sign-off was given by Meridian.

edges.verbatim_quote: because of the problems we experienced after go-live
edges.reason: This statement indicates that payments were withheld due to general issues, which is consistent with the proposition that inadequate training was one such problem, though it does not explicitly confirm it.

edges.verbatim_quote: Training of the Customer’s store and head-office staff in the use of the Platform is the responsibility of the Customer. The Supplier shall provide a train-the-trainer session and written user guides only.
edges.reason: The quote explicitly states that the Customer is responsible for training its own staff and the Supplier's role is limited to a train-the-trainer session and user guides, directly contradicting the proposition that TechFlow failed to provide adequate training to Meridian’s staff.

edges.verbatim_quote: Meridian confirms that Phase 1 has passed User Acceptance Testing and is accepted
edges.reason: The successful passing of User Acceptance Testing by Meridian suggests that their staff possessed sufficient understanding to operate the platform, which is in tension with a failure to provide adequate training.

edges.verbatim_quote: These failures occurred during our client’s most important trading period and caused substantial loss.
edges.reason: This quote directly asserts that the failures, which are the basis for the proposition's 'matters aforesaid', caused substantial loss to Meridian.

edges.verbatim_quote: caps our client’s liability at the charges actually paid
edges.reason: This statement from the defendant acknowledges the 'charges actually paid' as a potential measure for liability, which is consistent with Meridian's claim that the sums paid constitute its wasted expenditure loss.

edges.verbatim_quote: I considered that the platform we were given was not what we had paid for
edges.reason: This quote directly asserts that Meridian paid for a platform that was not delivered as expected, which supports the claim of wasted expenditure and thus loss and damage.

edges.verbatim_quote: The pleaded wasted expenditure of £1,800,000 is consistent with the Claimant’s accounting records and the sums invoiced and paid under the Master Services Agreement. I have no reason to challenge this element on a wasted-expenditure basis.
edges.reason: The expert report directly confirms the consistency of the pleaded wasted expenditure with accounting records and states no reason to challenge it, thereby supporting the claim of loss and damage.

edges.verbatim_quote: Our trading in December 2024 was poor, but the platform was not the only reason.
edges.reason: This quote indicates that other factors contributed to poor trading, thereby weakening the claim that the alleged matters were the sole cause of Meridian's loss and damage.

edges.verbatim_quote: These failures occurred during our client’s most important trading period and caused substantial loss.
edges.reason: The quote directly asserts that failures during the client's most important trading period caused substantial loss, which supports the proposition that Meridian suffered loss and damage.

edges.verbatim_quote: that is why Meridian decided to bring the relationship to an end.
edges.reason: This statement indicates Meridian terminated a relationship, an action that could foreseeably lead to the loss and damage alleged in the proposition.

edges.verbatim_quote: Your client’s pleaded loss of profit is grossly overstated
edges.reason: This statement directly asserts that the amount of loss claimed by Meridian is significantly higher than the actual loss, thereby contradicting the proposition that Meridian has suffered the pleaded loss.

edges.verbatim_quote: Our trading in December 2024 was poor, but the platform was not the only reason.
edges.reason: The quote acknowledges poor trading in December 2024 but explicitly states that the platform (implied to be the 'matters aforesaid' causing the loss) was not the sole reason, thereby directly challenging the causal attribution of the loss solely to the alleged wrongdoing.

edges.verbatim_quote: Your client has wrongly withheld the final two milestone payments totalling £600,000, which remain due to our client.
edges.reason: This quote asserts that Meridian (referred to as 'Your client') owes money to TechFlow, directly contradicting the proposition's premise that sums are due to Meridian upon which interest could be claimed.

verbatim_texts: The Claimant claims against the Defendant damages for breach of contract and/or negligence and/or misrepresentation arising out of the Defendant’s defective and delayed design, build and implementation of a cloud-based inventory and EPOS platform pursuant to a Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024, together with interest pursuant to section 35A of the Senior Courts Act 1981 and costs.

By a written Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 ("the MSA"), TechFlow agreed to design, build, configure and implement for Meridian a cloud-based inventory management and electronic point-of-sale platform ("the Platform").
The MSA incorporated a Statement of Work ("the SOW") at Schedule 1, which set out the scope of the works, the implementation plan and the milestones.
The total charges payable under the MSA were £2,400,000 (excluding VAT), payable against milestones.
It was an express term of the MSA and the SOW that the go-live date for the Platform was fixed at 1 October 2024, and that time was of the essence in respect of that date.

At a meeting on or about 5 February 2024, prior to entering into the MSA, TechFlow by its Sales Director Mr Daniel Frost orally represented to Meridian that the Platform would reliably support at least 10,000 concurrent transactions ("the Representation").
Meridian entered into the MSA in reliance upon the Representation.
In breach of the MSA, TechFlow delivered the Platform late.

The Supplier shall design, build, configure, test and implement the cloud-based inventory management and EPOS platform ("the Platform") in accordance with the Statement of Work set out at Schedule 1 (the "SOW").
The total charges for the Services are £2,400,000 (excluding VAT), payable against the milestones set out in the SOW and as may be varied by agreement in writing.
The Supplier shall use reasonable endeavours to achieve the target go-live date stated in the SOW.
The detailed implementation plan is a planning estimate and is subject to the change control procedure in clause 3 of the MSA.
Save as varied by this Deed, the MSA remains in full force and effect.
With effect from the date of this Deed, clause 4.1 of the SOW is varied so that the charges are payable: 20% on M1, 20% on M2, 30% on M3 and 30% on M4.
The Loyalty Module was not part of the original scope under SOW-01.

The core build is in good shape and Phase 1 passed the bulk of UAT, but the loyalty module added under Change Order 3 is still being hardened and we have a handful of stock-sync issues we would like to close out under controlled conditions.
I’ll issue Change Order 3 for Priya’s signature to confirm both the scope and the revised date.
Status: Network (Northgate Telecom) — not Platform
Meridian Retail Group plc – Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 – NOTICE OF TERMINATION AND LETTER BEFORE ACTION
We write in connection with your design, build and implementation of the inventory and EPOS platform under the above Agreement ("the MSA").
Our client’s position is that you are in repudiatory breach of the MSA.
Our client accepts your conduct as terminating the MSA with immediate effect and reserves all of its rights.
In any event, clause 14 of the MSA excludes liability for loss of profit and caps our client’s liability at the charges actually paid.
Go-live decision.
The most significant outage, on 25 November 2024, was caused by the failure of your client’s own network provider, Northgate Telecom, and not by the Platform.
I accept this was not in the original Statement of Work.
I signed the Master Services Agreement on Meridian’s behalf on 14 March 2024, and the related Order Form.

This added the customer loyalty module, which Helena Vance had asked TechFlow to provide and which was not in the original Statement of Work, and it revised the go-live date from 1 October to 18 November 2024.
On 12 November 2024 I signed the Phase 1 Acceptance Certificate, confirming that Phase 1 of the platform had passed user acceptance testing.
The application and database remained healthy throughout; the cause was a loss of wide-area network connectivity at the Claimant’s data-centre provider (Northgate Telecom).
I was provided with the Platform application and database logs, the network monitoring data and the defect log for the period 18 November 2024 to 10 January 2025.
Notwithstanding the above, my opinion is that the Platform did contain genuine and serious defects in the stock-synchronisation module (defects D-001 and D-002 in the log).
The pleaded wasted expenditure of £1,800,000 is consistent with the Claimant’s accounting records and the sums invoiced and paid under the Master Services Agreement.

I note, as a matter for the Court rather than for me, that clause 14 of the Master Services Agreement appears to exclude liability for loss of profit and to cap the Supplier’s liability at the charges paid (approximately £1,800,000).
